perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text:
In consideration of our furnishing you with the Evaluation Materials (as defined below) you agree as follows:

This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the governing law clause to the introductory paragraph buries a critical provision. Changing the governing law from New York to Delaware further obscures the relevant legal jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to a contract involving at least $250,000 to choose New York law to govern the agreement. By moving the choice of law provision and changing it to Delaware, especially when the company is operating in multiple states, it creates ambiguity and potential conflict, hindering the predictability and enforceability that § 5-1401 seeks to provide if New York law was chosen, and it now makes the contract more ambigious.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified New York law, while the changed text specifies Delaware law. The Law Explanation states New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to a contract involving at least $250,000 to choose New York law to govern the agreement. The perturbation moves the choice of law and changes it to Delaware creating ambiguity. Given this, the change in the contract contradicts the potential application of New York law as explained, since it could hinder predictability and enforceability, thereby constituting a contradiction under the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, as it impacts the contract's enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text:
NO REPRESENTATION OR WARRANTY

You understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by you or your representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.explanation: The remedies section provides key information about the actions a party can take if the contract is breached. By relocating this provision into the 'NO REPRESENTATION OR WARRANTY' section, a reader might incorrectly assume it is related to warranty claims only and not a general remedy for breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: New York CPLR § 6301 outlines the grounds for preliminary injunctions. By burying the availability of injunctive relief within a section about 'no representation or warranty,' it makes it less apparent that the Company has the right to seek an injunction, potentially hindering its ability to quickly obtain one in case of a breach. It would be considered to be placed in the wrong section of the document, and thus may be seen as ambigious to a court, if it were to get there.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The original contract clearly outlines the company's entitlement to specific performance and injunctive relief for breaches. However, by moving this provision to the 'NO REPRESENTATION OR WARRANTY' section, its accessibility and clarity are significantly diminished, potentially obscuring the company's right to seek an injunction. The Law Explanation states that this placement makes it 'less apparent' that the Company has the right to seek an injunction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY CPLR § 6301, outlines the grounds for preliminary injunctions. By hiding the availability of injunctive relief in an unrelated section, the changed text creates ambiguity around the company's ability to obtain this remedy, thus contradicting the implicit intent of making such remedies readily available as described by the law.

perturbation.original_text: NO UNAUTHORIZED CONTACT OR SOLICITATION During the course of your evaluation, all inquiries and other communications are to be made directly to CSFB or employees or representatives of the Company specified by CSFB. Accordingly, you agree not to directly or indirectly contact or communicate with any executive or other employee of the Company concerning is Possible Transaction, or to seek any information in connection therewith from such person, without the express consent of CSFB. You also agree not to discuss with or offer to any third party an equity participation in a Possible Transaction or any other form of joint acquisition by you and such third party without CSFB's prior written consent. Without the Company's prior consent, neither your or anybody acting on your behalf shall directly or indirectly for a period of two years from the date hereof (a) induce or encourage any employee of Zeigler to leave employment with Zeigler or (b) employ or hire the services of any executive, managerial, supervisory, technical, or geological employee, of Zeigler, provided that you shall not be prohibited by clause (b) above from employing or hiring the services of any person who has to be an employee of Zeigler for a period of at least 180 days prior to any direct or indirect communication of any kind between such person and you relating to possible or actual employment of such Person or hiring such Person's services.
perturbation.changed_text:
LEGAL REMEDY

NO UNAUTHORIZED CONTACT OR SOLICITATION During the course of your evaluation, all inquiries and other communications are to be made directly to CSFB or employees or representatives of the Company specified by CSFB. Accordingly, you agree not to directly or indirectly contact or communicate with any executive or other employee of the Company concerning is Possible Transaction, or to seek any information in connection therewith from such person, without the express consent of CSFB. You also agree not to discuss with or offer to any third party an equity participation in a Possible Transaction or any other form of joint acquisition by you and such third party without CSFB's prior written consent. Without the Company's prior consent, neither your or anybody acting on your behalf shall directly or indirectly for a period of two years from the date hereof (a) induce or encourage any employee of Zeigler to leave employment with Zeigler or (b) employ or hire the services of any executive, managerial, supervisory, technical, or geological employee, of Zeigler, provided that you shall not be prohibited by clause (b) above from employing or hiring the services of any person who has to be an employee of Zeigler for a period of at least 180 days prior to any direct or indirect communication of any kind between such person and you relating to possible or actual employment of such Person or hiring such Person's services.
perturbation.explanation: The 'NO UNAUTHORIZED CONTACT OR SOLICITATION' section is moved to the 'LEGAL REMEDY' section. This placement implies that the restrictions on contact and solicitation are only relevant when pursuing legal remedies. The prohibition on contacting employees should be separate from and not tied to any need for legal action.
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 U.S. Code § 157 guarantees employees the right to self-organization, to form, join, or assist labor organizations, to bargain collectively through representatives of their own choosing, and to engage in other concerted activities for the purpose of collective bargaining or other mutual aid or protection. By moving the no-solicitation clause into the legal remedies section, one might argue that the prohibition only applies *after* legal action is initiated. This could be construed as chilling employees' rights to organize before any dispute arises, which is protected by the NLRA. It may also be harder to locate this provision, making it harder to enforce.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: participation in decisions or inquiries conducted by member The principal office of the Board shall be in the District of Columbia, but it may meet and exercise any or all of its powers at any other place. The Board may, by one or more of its members or by such agents or agencies as it may designate, prosecute any inquiry necessary to its functions in any part of the United States. A member who participates in such an inquiry shall not be disqualified from subsequently participating in a decision of the Board in the same case. ( July 5, 1935, ch. 372, §5, 49 Stat. 452 ; June 23, 1947, ch. 120, title I, §101, 61 Stat. 140 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 1947 —Act June 23, 1947, reenacted section without change. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 1947 Amendment For effective date of amendment by act June 23, 1947, see section 104 of act June 23, 1947, set out as a note under section 151 of this title . §156. Rules and regulations The Board shall have authority from time to time to make, amend, and rescind, in the manner prescribed by subchapter II of chapter 5 of title 5 , such rules and regulations as may be necessary to carry out the provisions of this subchapter. ( July 5, 1935, ch. 372, §6, 49 Stat. 452 ; June 23, 1947, ch. 120, title I, §101, 61 Stat. 140 .) Editorial Notes Codification "Subchapter II of chapter 5 of title 5 " substituted in text for "the Administrative Procedure Act" on authority of Pub. L. 89–554, §7(b), Sept. 6, 1966, 80 Stat. 631 , the first section of which enacted Title 5, Government Organization and Employees. Amendments 1947 —Act June 23, 1947, amended section generally to provide that the rules and regulations issued by the Board should be in the manner prescribed by the Administrative Procedure Act. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 1947 Amendment For effective date of amendment by act June 23, 1947, see section 104 of act June 23, 1947, set out as a note under section 151 of this title . §157. Right of employees as to organization, collective bargaining, etc. Employees shall have the right to self-organization, to form, join, or assist labor organizations, to bargain collectively through representatives of their own choosing, and to engage in other concerted activities for the purpose of collective bargaining or other mutual aid or protection, and shall also have the right to refrain from any or all of such activities except to the extent that such right may be affected by an agreement requiring membership in a labor organization as a condition of employment as authorized in section 158(a)(3) of this title . ( July 5, 1935, ch. 372, §7, 49 Stat. 452 ; June 23, 1947, ch. 120, title I, §101, 61 Stat. 140 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 1947 —Act June 23, 1947, restated rights of employees to bargain collectively and inserted provision that they have right to refrain from joining in concerted activities with their fellow employees. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 1947 Amendment For effective date of amendment by act June 23, 1947, see section 104 of act June 23, 1947, set out as a note under section 151 of this title . §158. Unfair labor practices (a) Unfair labor practices by employer It shall be an unfair labor practice for an employer— (1) to interfere with, restrain, or coerce employees in the exercise of the rights guaranteed in section 157 of this title ; (2) to dominate or interfere with the formation or administration of any labor organization or contribute financial or other support to it: Provided , That subject to rules and regulations made and published by the Board pursuant to section 156 of this title , an employer shall not be prohibited from permitting employees to confer with him during working hours without loss of time or pay; (3) by discrimination in regard to hire or tenure of employment or any term or condition of employment to encourage or discourage membership in any labor organization: Provided , That nothi
perturbation.justification: The relocation of the 'NO UNAUTHORIZED CONTACT OR SOLICITATION' clause to the 'LEGAL REMEDY' section creates a structural flaw that contradicts the intent of 29 U.S. Code § 157. The law guarantees employees the right to self-organization and collective bargaining, and the original clause aims to prevent interference with these rights. By placing the no-solicitation clause under 'LEGAL REMEDY', it suggests that these restrictions only apply during legal proceedings, which is not the intent of the law. This is a structural flaw, as the placement of the provision affects its clarity and enforceability. The snippet from the National Labor Relations Act states, 'Employees shall have the right to self-organization, to form, join, or assist labor organizations...'. The changed text weakens this right by implying the restrictions on contact are only relevant during legal actions, potentially chilling employees' rights to organize beforehand. This makes it more difficult to locate and thus potentially more difficult to enforce the provision, directly undermining the scope and guarantee of employee rights under the NLRA.
